content.plain_english: This week in Year 5 maths, your child is working on Converting units. The learning focuses on: use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling; measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres; and calculate and compare the area of squares and rectangles. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Look for real-life examples around the house — a map showing distances, a measuring jug with ml and pints, a food label in grams and kg. Ask your child to read the measurements and convert between them. Road signs in miles are another good context.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling?
Was it easy or tricky to measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: Change a measurement from one unit to another — e.g. 150 cm = 1.5 m.
The modern measurement system using metres, kilograms, litres, etc.
An older system using miles, pounds, pints, etc.
When two measurements have the same value but use different units.
A way of comparing quantities — e.g. 1 kg ≈ 2.2 pounds.

example_questions: A recipe uses 750 ml. How many litres is that?
content.times_table_tip: By Year 5 children should know all their times tables with speed and confidence. Ask quick-fire questions at dinner — mixing tables so they recall facts rather than just reciting sequences. Can they answer in under 3 seconds?
